text: Endoscopy revealed a large subepithelial mass 1 cm proximal to the gastroesophageal junction.
On endoscopic ultrasound, the lesion appeared hypoechoic and inhomogeneous, arising from the muscularis propria.
Fine needle aspiration was performed with spindle cells identified on cytology, consistent with a leiomyoma.
We began by first mobilizing the left lung.
The lung was deflated and tucked superiorly to obtain a clear operating view.
The esophagus was then mobilized and the lower third pulled up through the diaphragmatic hiatus.
We were careful to identify and protect the vagus nerve.
Stay sutures were inserted to help with retraction.
360 degree full mobilization of the esophagus was performed.
Myotomy was performed with the incision extended using scissors.
Bipolar diatomy
and hooked diatomy.
This revealed the leiomyoma.
Retraction sutures were inserted to facilitate the section of the leiomyoma, but as the lesion was quite soft,
the sutures cheese wire through.
A small breach in the mucosa was noted during the section.
This was marked with a stitch to be repaired later on.
The lesion was carefully dissected free using a mixture of sharp and blunt dissection facilitated by bipolar diatomy, scissors and gauze.
The lesion was noted to have multiple finger-like projections extending both longitudinally and horizontally around the esophagus, creating a horseshoe effect.
The specimen was then bagged and placed aside for removal at the end of the operation.
A nasogastric tube was then inserted under direct vision.
The breach in the mucosa was then stitched with 5-0 interrupted proline sutures.
The breech was closed horizontally to prevent narrowing of the esophageal lumen.
The muscle layer was then approximated with 4 O interrupted PDS sutures.
Fibrin glue was then applied over the side of the repair.
The lung was repositioned and the chest tube was inserted under direct vision.
The left lung was gradually re-inflated.
Total operative time was 4 hours.
